name: AI Tutor — High School Student Over a Semester
description: AI tutor working with a 10th grade student over a semester. Tracks subject strengths/gaps, learning style, misconceptions corrected, and grade trajectory.

turns.content: I'm Marcus, 10th grade. I need help with Algebra 2 and Chemistry. I'm good at reading and English but math kills me.
Got it, Marcus — 10th grade, strong in English/reading, struggling with math. Algebra 2 and Chemistry are the focus.
My teacher is Mr. Harrison for Algebra 2. We're starting polynomial functions next week.
Algebra 2 with Mr. Harrison, polynomial functions coming up.
In Chemistry I got a 54% on the first exam. We're covering stoichiometry and I keep getting the mole conversions wrong.
Chemistry alert: 54% on first exam, mole conversion errors in stoichiometry. That's the first gap to close.
I learn better with real-world examples and pictures. Not abstract formulas dumped on me.
Learning style: visual and concrete examples. Abstract formula dumps do not work for Marcus.

sessions.label: Algebra 2 deep dive and misconception fix

turns.content: I had a quiz on polynomial functions. Got 62%. I keep thinking FOIL works for trinomials — like (x+1)(x+2+3). My teacher marked it wrong.
Common misconception caught: FOIL only works for two binomials. For a binomial times a trinomial, distribute every term. Fixed on record.
OH. That makes sense actually. So FOIL is a shortcut, not a rule?
Exactly — FOIL is a mnemonic for a specific case (binomial × binomial). Underlying rule is the distributive property. Always apply that and FOIL appears naturally.
Chemistry: I got 78% on the stoichiometry retest. Mole conversions are clicking now.
Chemistry improvement: 54% → 78% on stoichiometry retest. Mole conversions resolved.
Mr. Harrison is out on paternity leave. We have a sub, Ms. Rivera. She teaches differently — more formula-heavy.
Context updated: Mr. Harrison on paternity leave, substitute Ms. Rivera. She uses a more formula-heavy approach — that's a known challenge for Marcus.
We started rational functions. I'm confused about vertical asymptotes — I keep thinking the function equals zero at the asymptote.
Misconception flagged: vertical asymptotes occur where the denominator equals zero, NOT where the function equals zero. The function is undefined there, not zero. Important distinction.
Chemistry midterm was 82%. Things are clicking.
Chemistry midterm: 82%. Solid upward trajectory from 54%.
Mr. Harrison is back. Ms. Rivera moved fast and now I'm behind on graphing rational functions.
Mr. Harrison returned. Marcus is behind on graphing rational functions from the sub period — catchup needed.
I have a test on this in 2 weeks. I'm really stressed. My parents are on my case about the Algebra grade — it's a C+ right now.
Algebra 2 current grade: C+. Test in 2 weeks on rational functions. High-stakes session needed.
We also started logarithms in parallel. I actually think I understand logarithms — they make more sense than polynomials to me.
Unexpected strength: Marcus is picking up logarithms well. More intuitive than polynomials for him.
Got an 87% on the rational functions test! Way better than expected.
Rational functions test: 87%. Big jump from C+ territory. Grade likely improving.
Final Algebra 2 grade was a B. Chemistry was a B+. Huge improvement from the start.
Semester final: Algebra 2 B, Chemistry B+. Started the semester with a 54% Chemistry exam and Algebra struggles.
Next semester I have Pre-Calc and I'm adding AP Environmental Science. I'm nervous about AP.
Next semester: Pre-Calc and AP Environmental Science. AP course is new territory — tracking.

queries.query: What are Marcus's final semester grades?
Who is Marcus's current Algebra 2 teacher?
queries.expected_answer: Mr. Harrison. He was on paternity leave mid-semester and covered by Ms. Rivera, but returned before the semester ended.
queries.trap: Mr. Harrison → Ms. Rivera → Mr. Harrison. Must return current teacher.

queries.query: What misconception did Marcus have about FOIL?
queries.expected_answer: Marcus thought FOIL worked for any polynomial multiplication, including binomial × trinomial. FOIL only applies to binomial × binomial. The underlying rule is the distributive property.

queries.query: What misconception did Marcus have about vertical asymptotes?
queries.expected_answer: Thought the function equals zero at a vertical asymptote. Correction: vertical asymptotes occur where the denominator is zero — the function is undefined there, not zero.

queries.query: What is Marcus's learning style?
queries.expected_answer: Visual learner who needs real-world concrete examples. Abstract formula presentations don't work for him.

queries.query: How did Marcus's Chemistry grade change over the semester?
queries.expected_answer: Started at 54% on the first exam (stoichiometry). Improved to 78% on the retest. Midterm: 82%. Final grade: B+.
queries.trap: Must trace the full arc across four data points, not just the final grade.

queries.query: Is mole conversion still a problem for Marcus?
queries.expected_answer: No. It was resolved in session 2 — went from 54% to 78% on the stoichiometry retest.
queries.trap: Session 1 flags mole conversion as a gap. Session 2 closes it. Must return resolved state.

queries.query: What subjects will Marcus take next semester?
What was Marcus's Algebra grade before the rational functions test?
queries.expected_answer: C+ heading into the rational functions test (session 4).
queries.trap: Grade improved to B after the test. Must recall the pre-test grade specifically.

queries.query: What unexpected math strength did Marcus discover?
queries.expected_answer: Logarithms — they clicked more naturally than polynomials for Marcus.

queries.query: What happened when the substitute teacher was teaching?
queries.expected_answer: Ms. Rivera moved faster and used a more formula-heavy approach — a known challenge for Marcus. He fell behind on graphing rational functions and needed catchup.

queries.query: What is Marcus good at in school?
queries.expected_answer: Reading and English. Math is his weaker subject.

queries.query: What was the Chemistry starting score and how did the student improve?
queries.expected_answer: Started at 54% on the first stoichiometry exam. Fixed mole conversion errors. Improved to 78% on retest, 82% on midterm, and finished with a B+ for the semester.
queries.trap: Requires connecting all Chemistry data points across 4 sessions into a coherent progression.

queries.query: What was the score on the rational functions test?
How should content be presented to Marcus given his learning style?
queries.expected_answer: Use real-world examples and visual aids. Avoid leading with abstract formulas. Build concrete intuition first, then formalize.

queries.query: What topics caused the most difficulty this semester?
queries.expected_answer: Polynomial functions (FOIL misconception), rational functions/graphing (fell behind during sub period), and initially stoichiometry in Chemistry. All were resolved by end of semester.
queries.trap: Must identify all problem areas and note their resolution status accurately.

queries.query: Did Mr. Harrison come back before the semester ended?
queries.expected_answer: Yes. He returned from paternity leave (session 4) and finished the semester with Marcus.

queries.query: What subject started with a failing score and ended with a B+?
queries.expected_answer: Chemistry — 54% on the first exam, B+ final grade.
queries.trap: Must connect starting score (54%) and final grade (B+) to identify the subject.

queries.query: What is the underlying mathematical rule that FOIL is a special case of?
queries.expected_answer: The distributive property. FOIL is a mnemonic shortcut for applying distribution to two binomials.

queries.query: What are Marcus's concerns going into next semester?
queries.expected_answer: Nervous about taking AP Environmental Science — AP coursework is new territory for him.
